title: Methods and systems for single molecule tracking in cell-free systems
abstract: The present disclosure relates to single molecule tracking methods, including high throughput methods, for analyzing molecules in cell-free samples. The present disclosure further provides systems for performing such methods.
claims:
Claims WHAT IS CLAIMED IS: 1. A method of identifying an interaction between a target molecule and a test molecule comprising: (a) contacting a cell-free sample comprising a plurality of the target molecule with a plurality of the test molecule; (b) tracking a plurality of the target molecules over time to provide a plurality of spatiotemporal trajectories and/or measurements of rotational motion; (c) analyzing the plurality of spatiotemporal trajectories and/or measurements of rotational motion to determine the target moleculeâs movement in the presence of the test molecule; and (d) comparing the target moleculeâ s movement obtained in (c) with a reference target moleculeâs movement, wherein the reference target moleculeâs movement is the movement of the target molecule in the absence of the test molecule; wherein a change in the target moleculeâs movement compared to the reference target moleculeâs movement indicates an interaction between the target molecule and the test molecule. 2. The method of claim 1, wherein the method is a high throughput method. 3. The method of claim 1 or 2, wherein the change in movement is calculated as a change in: (a) diffusion coefficient obtained from a maximum likelihood estimator; (b) geometric mean posterior diffusion coefficient; (c) median of the jump length distribution; (d) 3rd quartile of the jump length distribution; (e) median radius of gyration; (f) mean posterior diffusion coefficient; (g) mean squared displacement; (h) median bond angle; (i) spatiotemporal trajectory length; (j) anisotropy decay time; (k) spatial extent of detection; (l) number and wavelength of conjugated fluorescent labels; (m) occupancy in different diffusive states obtained from state arrays; (n) polarization of conjugated fluorescent labels; or (o) state occupation via inference. 4. The method of any one of claims 1-3, wherein the change in movement is calculated as a change in diffusion coefficient. 5. The method of any one of claims 1-4, wherein an increase or decrease in the target moleculeâs movement compared to the reference target moleculeâs movement indicates an interaction between the target molecule and the test molecule. 6. The method of any one of claims 1-5, wherein the interaction between the target molecule and the test molecule is reversible or irreversible. 7. The method of any one of claims 1-6, wherein the duration and/or reversibility of the target moleculeâs movement change compared to the duration and/or reversibility of the reference target moleculeâs movement change indicates an interaction between the target molecule and the test molecule. 8. The method of any one of claims 1-7, wherein the target molecule and/or test molecule is a component of a mixture comprising a bacterial extract, cell extract, tissue extract, plant extract, or animal extract. 9. The method of claim 8, wherein the bacterial extract, cell extract, tissue extract, plant extract, or animal extract is a lysate. 10. The method of any one of claims 1-9, wherein the target molecule and/or test molecule is a component of a mixture comprising serum, blood, and/or other biological samples. 11. The method of any one of claims 1-10, wherein the target molecule and/or test molecule is a component of a mixture comprising a buffer and glycerol. 12. The method of any one of claims 1-11, wherein the target molecule and/or the test molecule is an organic molecule less than IKDa. 13. The method of any one of claims 1-12, wherein the target molecule and/or the test molecule is selected from the group consisting of a peptide, a protein, a nucleic acid, a carbohydrate, and a lipid. 14. The method of claim 13, wherein the protein is selected from the group consisting of an antibody, a receptor, and an enzyme. 15. The method of claim 13 or 14, wherein the protein comprises a disordered domain and/or does not comprise a structured domain. 16. The method of claim 13, wherein the peptide is a ligand. 17. The method of any one of claims 1-11, wherein the target molecule and/or the test molecule is a nanomaterial or a synthetic polymer. I l l 18. The method of any one of claims 1-17, wherein the target molecule and/or the test molecule is labeled. 19. The method of any one of claims 1-18, wherein the target molecule and/or the test molecule is fluorescently labeled. 20. The method of any one of claims 1-19, wherein the target molecule and/or the test molecule is labeled with a fluorescent protein. 21. The method of any one of claims 1-20, wherein the plurality of target molecules and/or the plurality of test molecules are conjugated to a plurality of fluorescent labels. 22. The method of any one of claims 1-21, wherein the target molecule and/or the test molecule is labeled by conjugation to a synthetic nanomaterial or polymer. 23. The method of any one of claims 1-22, wherein the change in the target moleculeâs movement compared to the reference target moleculeâs movement indicates a direct or indirect interaction between the target molecule and the test molecule. 24. The method of any one of claims 1-23, wherein the interaction between the target molecule and the test molecule results in a conformational change in the target molecule, an increase in the mass of the target molecule, a change in the temperature stability of the target molecule, and/or an oligomerization of the target molecule. 25. The method of any one of claims 1-24, wherein the cell-free sample comprises a solution. 26. The method of claim 25, wherein the solution comprises a chaotropic agent, a carrier, a viscosity agent, or a combination thereof. 27. The method of claim 26, wherein the viscosity agent comprises glycerol. 28. The method of claim 27, wherein the solution comprises at least about 30% glycerol. 29. The method of any one of claims 25-28, wherein the solution comprises a temperature gradient, a chemical gradient, at least two phases, or a combination thereof. 30. The method of any one of claims 1-29, wherein the cell-free sample has a volume of about 0.1 pl to about 100 pl. 31. The method of any one of claims 1-30, wherein the change in movement is measured as a change in the diffusion coefficient of the target molecule as compared to the reference target moleculeâs diffusion coefficient, and the change is at least about 0.001%, at least about 0.005%, at least 0.01%, at least about 0.05%, at least about 0.1%, at least about 0.5%, at least about 1%, at least about 1.5%, at least about 2% or at least about 3%, 

at least about 4%, at least about 5%, at least about 6%, at least about 7%, at least about 8%, at least about 9% or at least about 10%. 32. A method for identifying an interaction between a test molecule and a target protein comprising a disordered domain comprising: (a) contacting a cell-free sample comprising a plurality of the target protein with a plurality of the test molecule; (b) tracking a plurality of the target protein over time to provide a plurality of spatiotemporal trajectories and/or measurements of rotational motion; (c) analyzing the plurality of spatiotemporal trajectories and/or measurements of rotational motion to determine the target proteinâs movement in the presence of the test molecule; and (d) comparing the target proteinâs movement obtained in (c) with a reference target proteinâs movement, wherein the reference target proteinâs movement is the movement of the target protein in the absence of the test molecule; wherein a change in the movement of the target protein comprising the disordered domain compared to the reference target proteinâs movement indicates an interaction between the target protein comprising the disordered domain and the test molecule. 33. The method of claim 32, wherein the method is a high throughput method. 34. The method of claim 32 or 33, wherein the change in movement is calculated as a change in: (a) diffusion coefficient obtained from a maximum likelihood estimator; (b) geometric mean posterior diffusion coefficient; (c) median of the jump length distribution; (d) 3rd quartile of the jump length distribution; (e) median radius of gyration; (f) mean posterior diffusion coefficient; (g) mean squared displacement; (h) median bond angle; (i) spatiotemporal trajectory length; (j) anisotropy decay time; (k) spatial extent of detection; (l) number and wavelength of conjugated fluorescent labels; (m) occupancy in different diffusive states obtained from state arrays; (n) polarization of conjugated fluorescent labels; or 

 (o) state occupation via inference. 35. The method of any one of claims 32-34, wherein the change in movement is calculated as a change in diffusion coefficient. 36. The method of any one of claims 32-35, wherein an increase or decrease in the movement of the target protein comprising the disordered domain compared to the reference target proteinâs movement indicates an interaction between the target protein comprising the disordered domain and the test molecule. 37. The method of any one of claims 32-36, wherein the interaction between the target protein comprising the disordered domain and the test molecule is reversible or irreversible. 38. The method of any one of claims 32-37, wherein the duration and/or reversibility of the movement change of the target protein comprising the disordered domain compared to the duration and/or reversibility of the reference target proteinâs movement change indicates an interaction between the target molecule and the test molecule. 39. The method of any one of claims 32-38, wherein the target protein comprising the disordered domain and/or the test molecule is a component of a mixture comprising a bacterial extract, cell extract, tissue extract, plant extract, or animal extract. 40. The method of claim 39, wherein the bacterial extract, cell extract, tissue extract, plant extract, or animal extract is a lysate. 41. The method of any one of claims 32-40, wherein the target protein comprising the disordered domain and/or the test molecule is a component of a mixture composed of serum, blood, and other biological samples. 42. The method of any one of claims 32-41, wherein the target protein comprising the disordered domain and/or the test molecule is a component of a mixture comprising a buffer and glycerol. 43. The method of any one of claims 32-42, wherein the target protein comprising the disordered domain is selected from the group consisting of an antibody, a receptor, and an enzyme. 44. The method of any one of claims 32-43, wherein the target protein comprising the disordered domain does not comprise a structured domain. 45. The method of claim 46 or 47, wherein the protein comprises a disordered domain and/or does not comprise a structured domain. 49. The method of claim 46, wherein the peptide is a ligand. 50. The method of any one of claims 32-49, wherein the target protein comprising the disordered domain and/or the test molecule is labeled. 51. The method of any one of claims 32-50, wherein the target protein comprising the disordered domain and/or the test molecule is fluorescently labeled. 52. The method of any one of claims 32-51, wherein the target protein and/or the test molecule is labeled with a fluorescent protein. 53. The method of any one of claims 32-52, wherein the target protein and/or the test molecule is labeled by conjugation of a synthetic nanomaterial or polymer. 54. The method of any one of claims 32-53, wherein the change in the movement of the target protein comprising the disordered domain compared to the reference target proteinâs movement indicates a direct or indirect interaction between the target protein comprising the disordered domain and the test molecule. 55. The method of any one of claims 32-54, wherein the interaction between the target protein comprising the disordered domain and the test molecule results in a conformational change in the target protein, a change in the temperature stability of the target protein, an increase in the mass of the target protein, and/or an oligomerization of the target protein. 56. The method of any one of claims 32-55, wherein the cell-free sample comprises a solution. 57. The method of claim 56, wherein the solution comprises a chaotropic agent, a carrier, a viscosity agent, or a combination thereof. 58. The method of claim 57, wherein the viscosity agent comprises glycerol. 59. The method of claim 58, wherein the solution comprises at least about 30% glycerol. 60. The method of any one of claims 56-59, wherein the solution comprises a temperature gradient, a chemical gradient, at least two phases, or a combination thereof. 61. The method of any one of claims 32-60, wherein the cell-free sample has a volume of about 0.1 pl to about 100 pl. 62. The method of any one of claims 32-61, wherein the change in movement is measured as a change in the diffusion coefficient of the target protein as compared to the reference target proteinâs diffusion coefficient, and the change is at least about 0.001%, at least about 0.005%, at least 0.01%, at least about 0.05%, at least about 0.1%, at least about 0.5%, at least about 1%, at least about 1.5%, at least about 2% or at least about 3%, at least about 4%, at least about 5%, at least about 6%, at least about 7%, at least about 8%, at least about 9% or at least about 10%. 63. A method for analyzing a target molecule in a test solution comprising: (a) tracking a plurality of the target molecules over time in the test solution to provide a plurality of spatiotemporal trajectories and/or measurements of rotational motion; (b) analyzing the plurality of spatiotemporal trajectories and/or measurements of rotational motion to determine the target moleculeâs movement in the test solution; and (c) comparing the target moleculeâ s movement obtained in (b) with a reference target moleculeâs movement, wherein the reference target moleculeâs movement is the movement of the target molecule in a reference solution; wherein: (i) a change in the target moleculeâs movement compared to the reference target moleculeâs movement indicates that the target molecule is affected by the test solution; or (ii) no change in the target moleculeâs movement compared to the reference target moleculeâs movement indicates that the target molecule is not affected by the test solution. 64. The method of claim 63, wherein the method is a high throughput method. 65. The method of claim 63 or 64, wherein the change in movement is calculated as a change in: (a) diffusion coefficient obtained from a maximum likelihood estimator; (b) geometric mean posterior diffusion coefficient; (c) median of the jump length distribution; (d) 3rd quartile of the jump length distribution; (e) median radius of gyration; (f) mean posterior diffusion coefficient; (g) mean squared displacement; (g) median bond angle; (i) spatiotemporal trajectory length; (j) anisotropy decay time; (k) spatial extent of detection; 

 (l) number and wavelength of conjugated fluorescent labels; (m) occupancy in different diffusive states obtained from state arrays; (n) polarization of conjugated fluorescent labels; or (o) state occupation via inference. 66. The method of any one of claims 63-65, wherein the change in movement is calculated as a change in diffusion coefficient. 67. The method of claim 82 or 83, wherein the viscosity agent is glycerol. 85. The method of any one of claims 63-84, wherein the test solution comprises a gradient. 86. The method of claim 85, wherein the gradient is a temperature gradient, a chemical gradient, or a combination thereof. 87. The method of any one of claims 63-86, wherein the test solution comprises at least two phases, or a combination thereof. 88. The method of any one of claims 63-87, wherein the interaction between the target molecule and the test solution results in a conformational change in the target molecule. 89. The method of any one of claims 63-88, wherein the interaction between the target molecule and the test solution is reversible or irreversible. 90. A method for determining the form of a target molecule comprising: (a) tracking a plurality of the target molecules over time in a cell-free sample to provide a plurality of spatiotemporal trajectories and/or measurements of rotational motion; (b) analyzing the plurality of spatiotemporal trajectories and/or measurements of rotational motion to determine the target moleculeâs movement; and (c) comparing the target moleculeâ s movement obtained in (b) with a reference target moleculeâs movement, wherein the reference target moleculeâs movement is the movement of one form of the target molecule; wherein: (i) a change in the target moleculeâs movement compared to the reference target moleculeâs movement indicates that the target molecule is of a different form than the reference target molecule; or (ii) no change in the target moleculeâs movement compared to the reference target moleculeâs movement indicates that the target molecule is of the same form as the reference target molecule. 91. The method of claim 90, wherein the method is a high throughput method. 92. The method of claim 90 or 91, wherein the change in movement is calculated as a change in: (a) diffusion coefficient obtained from a maximum likelihood estimator; (b) geometric mean posterior diffusion coefficient; (c) median of the jump length distribution; (d) 3rd quartile of the jump length distribution; (e) median radius of gyration; (f) mean posterior diffusion coefficient; (g) mean squared displacement; (h) median bond angle; (i) spatiotemporal trajectory length; (j) anisotropy decay time; (k) spatial extent of detection; (l) number and wavelength of conjugated fluorescent labels; (m) occupancy in different diffusive states obtained from state arrays; (n) polarization of conjugated fluorescent labels; or (o) state occupation via inference. 93. The method of any one of claims 90-92, wherein the change in movement is calculated as a change in diffusion coefficient. 94. The method of any one of claims 90-93, wherein the duration and/or reversibility of the target moleculeâs movement change compared to the duration and/or reversibility of the reference target moleculeâs movement change indicates that the target molecule is of a different form than the reference target molecule. 95. The method of any one of claims 90-96, wherein the target molecule and/or the reference target molecule is a component of a mixture composed of serum, blood, and other biological samples. 98. The method of claim 110, wherein the chaotropic agent is urea. 112. The method of claim 110 or 111, wherein the viscosity agent comprises glycerol. 113. The method of claim 112, wherein the solution comprises at least about 30% glycerol. 114. The method of any one of claims 109-113, wherein the solution comprises a temperature gradient, a chemical gradient, at least two phases, or a combination thereof. 115. The method of any one of claims 90-114, wherein the cell-free sample has a volume of about 0.1 pl to about 100 pl. 116. The method of any one of claims 90-115, wherein the change in movement is measured as a change in the diffusion coefficient of the target molecule as compared to the 

reference target moleculeâs diffusion coefficient, and the change is at least about 0.001%, at least about 0.005%, at least 0.01%, at least about 0.05%, at least about 0.1%, at least about 0.5%, at least about 1%, at least about 1.5%, at least about 2% or at least about 3%, at least about 4%, at least about 5%, at least about 6%, at least about 7%, at least about 8%, at least about 9% or at least about 10%. 117. The method of any one of claims 90-116, wherein the form of the target molecule is a post-translationally modified form of the target molecule, a wild type form of the target molecule, or a mutant form of the target molecule. 118. A method for identifying a test molecule that can distinguish between at least two target molecules comprising: (a) contacting a cell-free sample comprising a plurality of a first target molecule with a plurality of the test molecule; (b) tracking a plurality of the first target molecules over time to provide a plurality of spatiotemporal trajectories and/or measurements of rotational motion; (c) analyzing the plurality of spatiotemporal trajectories and/or measurements of rotational motion to determine the first target moleculeâs movement in the presence of the test molecule; (d) contacting a cell-free sample comprising a plurality of a second target molecule with a plurality of the test molecule; (e) tracking a plurality of the second target molecules over time to provide a plurality of spatiotemporal trajectories and/or measurements of rotational motion; (f) analyzing the plurality of spatiotemporal trajectories and/or measurements of rotational motion to determine the second target moleculeâs movement in the presence of the test molecule; and (g) comparing the first and second target moleculesâ movement obtained in (c) and (f); wherein a change in the movement of the first and second target molecules indicates that the test molecule can distinguish between the two target molecules. 119. The method of claim 118, wherein the method is a high throughput method. 120. The method of claim 118 or 119, wherein the change in movement is calculated as a change in: (a) diffusion coefficient obtained from a maximum likelihood estimator; (b) geometric mean posterior diffusion coefficient; (c) median of the jump length distribution; 

 (d) 3rd quartile of the jump length distribution; (e) median radius of gyration; (f) mean posterior diffusion coefficient; (g) mean squared displacement; (h) median bond angle; (i) spatiotemporal trajectory length; (j) anisotropy decay time; (k) spatial extent of detection; (l) number and wavelength of conjugated fluorescent labels; (m) occupancy in different diffusive states obtained from state arrays; (n) polarization of conjugated fluorescent labels; or (o) state occupation via inference. 121. The method of any one of claims 118-120, wherein the change in movement is calculated as a change in diffusion coefficient. 122. The method of any one of claims 118-121, wherein the duration and/or reversibility of the movement change of the first and second target molecules indicates that the test molecule can distinguish between the two target molecules. 123. The method of any one of claims 118-122, wherein the first target molecule, the second target molecule and/or the test molecule is a component of a mixture composed of a bacterial extract, cell extract, tissue extract, plant extract, or animal extract. 124. The method of claim 123, wherein the bacterial extract, cell extract, tissue extract, plant extract, or animal extract is a lysate. 125. The method of any one of claims 118-124, wherein the first target molecule, the second target molecule, and/or the test molecule is a component of a mixture composed of serum, blood, and other biological samples. 126. The method of any one of claims 118-125, wherein the first target molecule, the second target molecule, and/or the test molecule is a component of a mixture comprising a buffer and glycerol. 127. The method of any one of claims 118-127, wherein the first target molecule, the second target molecule, and/or the test molecule is selected from the group consisting of a peptide, a protein, a nucleic acid, a carbohydrate, and a lipid. 129. The method of claim 128, wherein the protein is selected from the group consisting of an antibody, a receptor, and an enzyme. 130. The method of claim 128 or 129, wherein the protein comprises a disordered domain and/or does not comprise a structured domain. 131. The method of claim 128, wherein the peptide is a ligand. 132. The method of any one of claims 118-126, wherein the first target molecule, the second target molecule, and/or the test molecule is a nanomaterial or a synthetic polymer. 133. The method of any one of claims 118-132, wherein the first target molecule, the second target molecule, and/or the test molecule is labeled. 134. The method of any one of claims 118-133, wherein the first target molecule, the second target molecule, and/or the test molecule is fluorescently labeled. 135. The method of any one of claims 118-135, wherein first target molecule, the second target molecule and/or the test molecule is labeled by conjugation of a synthetic nanomaterial or polymer. 137. The method of any one of claims 118-136, wherein the first and second target molecules are distinguished by a difference in their fluorescent labels. 138. The method of any one of claims 118-137, wherein the first target molecule and the second target molecule are related target molecules. 139. The method of claim 138, wherein at least one of the target molecules is a wild type form of the target molecule;wherein at least one of the target molecules is a mutant form of the target molecule; and/or wherein at least one of the target molecules is a post- translationally modified form of the target molecule. 140. The method of any one of claims 118-138, wherein the first target molecule and the second target molecule are the same or different conformational states of the target molecule; wherein the first target molecule and the second target molecule are homologs, orthologs or paralogs; and/or wherein the first target molecule and the second target molecule are unrelated target molecules. 141. The method of any one of claims 118-140, further comprising: contacting a cell-free sample comprising a plurality of a third target molecule with a plurality of the test molecule; tracking a plurality of the third target molecules over time to provide a third plurality of spatiotemporal trajectories and/or measurements of rotational motion; analyzing the third plurality of spatiotemporal trajectories and/or measurements of rotational motion to 

determine the movement of the third target molecules in the presence of the test molecule; and comparing the third target moleculeâs movement to the movements obtained in (c) and (f); wherein a change in the movement of the third target molecule relative to the first and second target molecules indicates that the test molecule can distinguish between the three target molecules. 142. A method for identifying one or more test molecules that induce a conformational change in a target molecule comprising: (a) contacting cell-free samples comprising a plurality of the target molecule with a plurality of test molecules, where each sample is contacted with a different test molecule; (b) tracking a plurality of the target molecules in each sample over time to provide a plurality of spatiotemporal trajectories and/or measurements of rotational motion; (c) analyzing the plurality of spatiotemporal trajectories and/or measurements of rotational motion to determine the target moleculeâs movement in the presence of each of the different test molecules; and (d) comparing the target moleculeâ s movement obtained in (c) with a reference target moleculeâs movement, wherein the reference target moleculeâs movement is the movement of the target molecule in the presence of a test molecule that does not induce a conformational change in the target molecule; wherein a change in the target moleculeâs movement compared to the reference target moleculeâs movement indicates an interaction between the target molecule and the one or more test molecules that induce a conformational change in the target molecule. 143. The method of claim 142, wherein the method is a high throughput method. 144. The method of claim 142 or 143, wherein the change in movement is calculated as a change in: (a) diffusion coefficient obtained from a maximum likelihood estimator; (b) geometric mean posterior diffusion coefficient; (c) the median of the jump length distribution; (b) 3rd quartile of the jump length distribution; (c) median radius of gyration; (d) mean posterior diffusion coefficient; (f) mean squared displacement; (g) median bond angle; (i) the spatiotemporal trajectory length; 

 (j) anisotropy decay time; (k) spatial extent of detection; (l) number and wavelength of conjugated fluorescent labels; (m) occupancy in different diffusive states obtained from state arrays; (n) polarization of conjugated fluorescent labels; or (o) state occupation via inference. 145. The method of any one of claims 142-144, wherein the change in movement is calculated as a change in diffusion coefficient. 146. The method of any one of claims 142-145, wherein the conformational change in the target molecule is associated with a change in the target moleculeâs temperature stability. 147. The method of any one of claims 142-146, wherein the interaction between the target molecule and the one or more test molecules is reversible or irreversible. 148. The method of any one of claims 142-147, wherein the duration and/or reversibility of the target moleculeâs movement change compared to the duration and/or reversibility of the reference target moleculeâ s movement change indicates an interaction between the target molecule and the one or more test molecules that induce a conformational change in the target molecule. 149.
